title: A method for ameliorating autoimmune disease by passive transfer of IVIg-primed leukocytes
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"itquh","text":"While strides have been made in the understanding of how intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) ameliorates autoimmune diseases by using mouse models of immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) and inflammatory arthritis, the precise cellular target of IVIg has remained elusive. To determine which cell populations interact with IVIg upstream of the inhibition of phagocytosis, we have developed a method for ‘priming’ specific leukocyte populations with IVIg to study their potential role in ameliorating ITP upon passive transfer, which we describe herein. Using this technique, we found that splenic CD11c+ dendritic cells reacted with IVIg, or several IVIg mimetic regimes, were primed to ameliorate ITP upon transfer to thrombocytopenic mice. We anticipate that this novel methodology could also be used to determine the mechanism of action of IVIg in other autoimmune diseases in addition to ITP.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"drxpl","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jwulj","text":"\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rwfec","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zsgo4","text":"Introduction\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"et9zk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"egq3w","text":"\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"yhmwj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qdpl5","text":"The precise mechanism of action of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) in the amelioration of autoimmune diseases such as immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) has, until now, been difficult to ascertain (1,2). Studies using gene knockout mice have demonstrated that in the treatment of murine ITP, IVIg requires the expression of the inhibitory receptor FcγRIIB (3,4) and that IVIg requires CSF-1-dependent macrophages to increase FcγRIIB expression on  CSF-1-independent macrophages (5). IVIg has also been demonstrated to affect the function and maturation of dendritic cells in vitro (6,7). However, the precise cellular target(s) of IVIg has remained, until recently, an enigma. In an attempt to answer these questions, we have developed a method for isolating and ‘priming’ splenic leukocytes or specific splenic cell populations with i) IVIg, ii) soluble immune complexes (sIC) or iii) antigen-specific antibodies plus crosslinking antibodies. Splenic white cells are used for priming as is (whole leukocytes) or purified into CD11b negative and positive or CD11c negative and positive populations using magnetic bead-coupled monoclonal antibodies. Cells are then incubated with IVIg, sIC or antigen-specific antibodies (as above) at 37oC for 30 min, washed and injected into mice one day prior to injection of anti-platelet antibody to induce ITP (8). This method avoids the actual administration of IVIg and allows the researcher to isolate specific cell populations, prime them and passively transfer them to thrombocytopenic mice in order to determine their potential role in IVIg function. Recently, using this technique we found activating Fcγ receptors on CD11c+ dendritic cells to be the target of IVIg and IVIg mimetics in the amelioration of ITP in our mouse model (9). We anticipate that this new technique could also be used to determine the mechanism of action of IVIg in other models of autoimmune disease.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":569,"length":8},{"style":"sup","offset":1233,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Determine the volume of IVIg required by taking into account the number of experimental conditions and the number of mice per experimental condition (see Step 3). Use a likewise prepared BSA solution as a control.
Remove the required length of the dialysis tubing (approximately 15-17 cm for 3-4 mL) and rinse in distilled H 2 0.
Transfer the desired volume of IVIg or BSA solution into dialysis tube, tie the ends of the tubing, and transfer the tube into sterile beaker containing sterile PBS (pH 7.2).
Stir in a beaker overnight at 4 o C.
Remove spleens from appropriate mice and mechanically disrupt in 5 mL of culture medium and filter through a 70-μm nylon cell strainer.
Lyse RBC using 1mL of ACK buffer for 5 min.
Wash cells 2x in RPMI-1640.
Alternatively, total splenic leukocytes may be replaced with specific cell type. Cell type selected will depend on the experimental set up.
Prepare CD11c+ and CD11c- cell fractions from splenic leukocytes by magnetic separation using the mouse CD11c positive selection kit according to the manufacturer's instructions.
Disrupt spleen in RPMI-1640 containing 43 U/mL collagenase IV for 20 minutes at 37 o C.
Further disrupt the spleen and add EDTA (1mM final concentration) for 5 minutes at 37 o C and filter the spleen preparation through a 70 μm nylon cell strainer.
Wash splenic leukocytes with CD11c purification buffer.
Resuspend the splenic leukocytes in CD11c purification buffer to 2x10 8 nucleated cells/mL and incubate with CD11c-PE at 15 μL/mL for 15 min at 22 o C.
After washing, incubate the splenic leukocytes in PE selection cocktail (100 μL cocktail/mL of cells) for 15 minutes at 22 o C.
Add magnetic particles (50 μL/mL cells) and incubate for 10 min at 22 o C, followed by separation using a magnet.
Collect the supernatant as the CD11c- cell fraction.
Remove the tube from the magnet, resuspend the cells as CD11c+ fraction, wash 1x in RPMI-1640.
Prepare CD11b+ and CD11b- cell fractions using CD11b immunomagnetic beads according to the manufacturer's instructions.
Prepare a single-cell suspension by disrupting the spleen in RPMI-1640 and filter through a 70-μm nylon cell strainer.
After RBC lysis using ACK buffer and washing using RPMI-1640, resuspend cells (10 8 /mL) in CD11b purification buffer containing CD11b microbeads (10 μL/10 7 ) cells).
Incubate for 15 minutes at 4 o C.
Wash the cells in CD11b purification buffer and apply to MACS column.
Collect the effluent as CD11b- cell fraction.
Remove the column from the magnet and flush the cells from the column as CD11b+ fraction.
Incubate splenic leukocytes (1.4x10 6 /mL) with 18 mg/mL IVIg or BSA for 30 min at 37 o C. For CD11c+ or CD11b+ cells use 1.4x10 5 /mL, and for CD11c- or CD11b- cells use 1.4x10 6 /mL.
Wash cells 2x in RPMI-1640.
Resuspend in culture medium to 5x10 6 /mL.
FcγRIIIA crosslinking: Incubate splenic leukocytes (2x10 7 /mL) with antibody 2.4G2 (2.5 μg/mL) or normal rat IgG (2.5 μg/mL) for 15 min at 22 o C in culture medium.
Wash cells 1x in RPMI-1640.
Incubate cells (2x10 7 /mL) with goat F(ab')2 anti-rat IgG (25 μg/mL) for 30 min at 37 o C.
Wash 2x in RPMI-1640.
Resuspend in culture medium to 5x10 6 /mL.
PIR-A/B crosslinking: Incubate splenic leukocytes (4x10 6 /mL) with 6C1 (10 μg/mL) or normal rat IgG (10 μg/mL) for 15 min at 22 o C in culture medium.
Wash cells 1x in RPMI-1640.
Incubate cells (4x10 6 /mL) with goat F(ab')2 anti-rat IgG (25 μg/mL) for 30 min at 37 o C.
Wash 2x in RPMI-1640.
Resuspend in culture medium to 5x10 6 /mL.
Soluble immune complexes (sIC): Dissolve OVA in PBS, pH 7.2 at 5 mg/mL.
Prepare sIC by incubating 1 mg of ovalbumin with 50 μg monoclonal anti-ovalbumin or normal mouse IgG as a control for 30 min at 22 o C.
Incubate splenic leukocytes (5x10 6 /mL) with sIC for 30 min at 37 o C.
Wash 2x in RPMI-1640.
Resuspend in culture medium to 5x10 6 /mL.
Inject 200 μL of cell suspension (5x10 6 /mL) into the tail vein of recipient mice (Day 0).
Inject each mouse i.p. with 2 μg anti-platelet antibody (MWReg30) in 200 μL PBS, pH 7.2 (Day 1).
Bleed mice on Day 2 for platelet enumeration as described (8,10,11).

It takes 12-16 hrs to prepare dialyzed IVIg or BSA. Cell preparation requires from 2-3 hrs depending on whether total splenic leukocytes or selected cell populations are used. Priming with IVIg requires 1 hr.
4 d
CRITICAL STEP Cell suspension should be mixed well immediately before injection into mice.
See Reference (9).
Intravenous immunoglobulin ameliorates ITPgamma via activating Fc receptors on dendritic cells by Siragam, V. et al. published in Nature Medicine on 23 May, 2006 https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/nm1416
creator: Protocols Importer
